misconceptionSummary: Students think the perimeter formula changes between rectangles and parallelograms.

visuals.ruleText: Rectangles and parallelograms share P = 2(a + b) because both have two pairs of equal opposite sides
visuals.subText: The right angles in a rectangle do not affect the perimeter calculation

steps.narration: The perimeter formula is identical for rectangles and parallelograms because both shapes share the same property: two pairs of equal opposite sides. Angles are irrelevant to perimeter — only the side lengths matter.
Walking around a rectangle you traverse each length twice and each width twice. Adding a length and a width gives one traverse of each — multiplying by two accounts for both opposite sides.
If the perimeter and one side are known, find the other. Half the perimeter is the sum of both side lengths: fifteen. Subtract the known side: fifteen minus nine equals six centimetres.

visuals.label: Formula works in reverse: half the perimeter minus one side

steps.narration: The formula is fully reversible. Know any two of the three values — perimeter, length, width — and you can find the third.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using a different method for parallelograms. Both rectangles and parallelograms have two pairs of equal opposite sides — P = 2(a + b) works for both.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Perimeter depends only on side lengths, not angles. Whether the shape is a rectangle or a parallelogram, add the two different side lengths and double.
